02_1.title: 1.  Introduction to NPS
02_1.content:
# 1.  Introduction to NPS  

**Source:** <https://zerodha.com/varsity/chapter/national-pension-system-nps/>  

---

If you’ve clicked on this page, give yourself a little high‑five (or a virtual one if you’re working from home). You’re part of the **tiny minority** that actually thinks about retirement while still being able to afford a latte. Most people treat “retirement planning” like it’s a scary movie—something they’d rather not watch until the credits roll and the popcorn’s gone.

In the world of financial education we’re constantly shouting, “Plan for the gray years!” but the crowd usually shushes us and pretends the future is a mystical realm where money grows on trees. By the time the reality‑check alarm goes off, many are frantically Googling “How do I retire without selling a kidney?”  

Our mission (yes, the one with the cape) is to make retirement concepts as easy to digest as a plate of nachos—layered, tasty, and with a side of salsa‑level fun. Even if we shift the needle by a millimetre, we’ll call it a win and pop the champagne (later, when we’re old enough to enjoy it responsibly).

In this module, I—Satya Sontanam—will break down the **key features of the National Pension System (NPS)**, India’s flagship retirement‑savings product. Think of NPS as the answer to the classic “*where should I park my retirement money?*” question. The “*how much should I save?*” part is still up to you, so grab a calculator or one of the free retirement‑planning tools floating around the internet and get your numbers in line. (Our blog, videos, and a few other hidden gems are just a click away if you need a cheat sheet.)

---

### 1.1 – NPS: A product to overcome our biases  

NPS is basically a **personal pension piggy‑bank** you feed during your earning years. The aim? Grow a tidy nest‑egg that hands you a steady stream of cash once you decide to retire your 9‑to‑5 grind.

Originally, NPS was a perk reserved for government employees. In 2009, the Indian government decided to spread the love and opened it up to **every citizen** under the “All‑Citizen” model. So, whether you’re a resident Indian, an NRI, 18‑year‑old fresh out of school, or a 70‑year‑old who just discovered TikTok, you can hop on board.

The scheme is overseen by the **Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority (PFRDA)**—the watchdog that makes sure the pension pool isn’t a free‑for‑all. But don’t mistake “government‑run” for “government‑guaranteed.” Unlike the classic government pension that promises a fixed amount, NPS is **market‑linked**. Your retirement corpus depends on two things:  

1. **How much you put in**, and  
2. **How well the underlying investments perform**.

In other words, it behaves like a mutual fund or a direct equity portfolio—no magical safety net, just the usual market ups and downs.

You could chase the same goal with a mutual fund, a direct stock basket, or even a SIP in a traditional PPF. NPS isn’t the *only* road to a happy retirement, but it does have a few quirks that make it stand out:

| Feature | Why It’s Cool (or Cruel) |
|---------|--------------------------|
| **Discipline‑enforcing rules** | You can’t just waltz in, pull out the cash, and buy a yacht the night before you turn 60. |
| **Minimum contribution** | If you skip the ₹1,000 annual minimum, your account goes into “hibernation.” To revive it you must pay the missed amount **plus a penalty**. No ceiling on the maximum, so you can go big if you wish. |
| **Withdrawal restrictions** | Money is locked until retirement (or 15 years under the MSF, see below). Even emergencies only allow a **partial** withdrawal, keeping you from impulsively blowing the whole stash on a “once‑in‑a‑lifetime” vacation. |
| **Mandatory annuity** | At 60, you get up to **60 %** of the corpus as a lump‑sum; the remaining **minimum 40 %** must be bought as an annuity (a product that pays you a regular pension). This stops you from spending the whole pot in one go—think of it as a “financial marriage” that forces you to stay together for life. |
| **Asset‑class choice** | You decide the split: e.g., 70 % equity, 20 % government bonds, 10 % corporate bonds. Not sure? The auto‑choice (age‑based) does the math for you. |
| **MSF (Multiple Scheme Framework)** | Launched **September 2025**, lets you allocate **100 %** to a pure equity fund if you’re feeling adventurous. |
| **Low cost & tax perks** | Management fees range **0.03 %–0.09 %** (new MSF schemes capped at **0.3 %** of AUM). That’s cheaper than most active mutual funds. Tax‑wise, NPS is an **EEE** (Exempt‑Exempt‑Exempt) instrument: up to **₹2  lakh** of your contribution is deductible under the old tax regime, earnings grow tax‑free, and the lump‑sum withdrawal is also tax‑free. The annuity portion, however, is taxed like any other pension income. |

#### Minimum Contribution – The “Don’t Forget to Feed the Piggy‑Bank” Rule  
Most government‑linked savings vehicles enforce a **floor** contribution to instil habit. In NPS, if you don’t pony up at least **₹1,000** in a year, your account gets frozen tighter than a Netflix password after a password‑reset. Un‑freeze it by paying the missed amount **plus a penalty** (the exact penalty is set by the regulator). The upside? No **cap** on how much you can add, so high‑earners can pour in as much as they like.

#### Withdrawal Restrictions – The “No‑Touch‑Zone”  
Think of it like that “out of sight, out of mind” principle you use for junk food. Once the money is in NPS, it stays there until you hit the retirement age (or the 15‑year mark under the **Multiple Scheme Framework**). Even when you’re allowed a partial emergency withdrawal, it’s only a slice of the cake—not the whole bakery. This design protects you from those “I’m feeling stressed, I need to buy a yacht” moments.

#### Mandatory Annuity at Maturity – The “Retirement Rent‑Check”  
When you finally turn 60, the system hands you **up to 60 %** as a lump‑sum. The remaining **minimum 40 %** must be funneled into an annuity, which then pays you a **regular income for life**. The rationale? Prevent the “lottery‑winner‑spends‑everything‑in‑a‑week” syndrome. By forcing a steady‑pay stream, NPS makes sure you don’t out‑live your savings.

#### Ease of Managing the Account – The “DIY Portfolio” Feature  
Unlike EPF or PPF, NPS lets you **mix and match asset classes**: equities, government securities, corporate bonds, and even alternative assets. You can set a custom split (e.g., 70 % equity, 20 % G‑Sec, 10 % corporate bond) or let the system auto‑allocate based on your age—young folks get more equity, seniors get safer bonds. The 2025‑born **MSF** also gives you the freedom to go **100 % equity** if you’re a risk‑taker.

#### Low Cost & Tax Advantages – The “Cheap & Cheerful” Angle  
Management fees are a **whisper** compared to the roar of active fund charges: **0.03 %–0.09 %** (or up to **0.3 %** for new MSF schemes). That’s cheaper than a cup of premium coffee per year for most investors. On the tax front, NPS falls under the coveted **EEE** bucket:  

* **E** – You can claim up to **₹2  lakh** of contributions as a deduction under Section 80C (old regime).  
* **E** – The earnings (interest, capital gains) are **tax‑free** while they sit in the pot.  
* **E** – The **lump‑sum** (up to 60 % of corpus) at retirement is **tax‑free**.  

Only the **annuity portion** (the mandatory 40 %) is taxed as regular pension income.

---

### 1.2 – Long‑term commitment  

All those quirks—minimum contribution, lock‑in periods, mandatory annuity—aren’t there to **make your life harder**; they’re there to **beat our own human biases**. We’re naturally impulsive, we love instant gratification, and we often procrastinate on saving for a future we can’t yet see.

The government’s angle is also strategic: a populace that saves for its own old‑age reduces the pressure on public welfare schemes as the population ages. Hence the **low fees** and **tax breaks**—they’re incentives to get more people to lock away money for later.

Think of NPS as the **marriage** of your money to your future self. Just like a legal marriage imposes rules, ceremonies, and (sometimes) penalties for quick divorces, NPS imposes rules that keep you from bailing on your retirement plan at the first sign of a tempting sale on a new TV. The goal isn’t to be punitive; it’s to **protect** you from your own short‑term whims, ensuring a decent nest‑egg when you finally decide to trade your office chair for a recliner.

---

## Key Takeaways  

- **All‑Citizen Model** – Since 2009, NPS is open to every Indian citizen (resident or NRI) aged **18‑70**.  
- **No guaranteed government pension** – NPS is market‑linked; the payout depends on contributions and investment performance.  
- **Broad eligibility** – Anyone (resident or non‑resident) can join, regardless of employment status.  
- **Equity exposure** – Unlike EPF/PPF, NPS lets you allocate a chunk of your corpus to equities, giving you growth potential.  
- **Discipline‑driven design** – Minimum annual contribution (₹1,000), lock‑in until retirement (or 15 years under MSF), and a mandatory 40 % annuity ensure you stay the course.  
- **Cost‑effective** – Management fees **0.03 %–0.09 %** (MSF caps at **0.3 %**), making it one of the cheapest retirement vehicles in India.  
- **Tax‑friendly (EEE)** – Up to **₹2  lakh** deduction under the old regime, earnings grow tax‑free, lump‑sum withdrawal tax‑free; annuity income taxed as regular pension.  

Now that you’ve got the lay of the land, go ahead and **run the numbers**, experiment with the calculators, and decide whether NPS fits into your retirement‑planning cocktail. Remember: the earlier you start, the more time your money has to compound, and the fewer “I wish I’d started earlier” regrets you’ll have at 60. Cheers to a financially disciplined future!

03_2.title: 2.  NPS vs. other retirement plans
03_2.content:
# 2.  NPS vs. other retirement plans  

**Source:** <https://zerodha.com/varsity/chapter/nps-vs-other-retirement-plans/>

---  

> “There is a choice you have to make in everything you do. And you must always keep in mind the choice you make makes you.”  
> – John Wooden, legendary basketball coach (and apparently a secret guru of retirement planning)

---

## 2.1 – Retirement‑focused investments  

**Should you throw your hard‑earned cash into NPS?**  
That’s a decision only you can make after you’ve measured it against the other retirement‑savvy toys on the Indian market and checked whether it vibes with your life‑plan. So, grab a cuppa (or a cold beer, I won’t judge) and let’s stack up NPS against the other big‑players.

| **Plan** | **Nature** | **Minimum‑contribution rule** | **Withdrawal restrictions** | **Typical asset tilt** |
|----------|------------|------------------------------|-----------------------------|------------------------|
| **NPS** | Government‑backed, tiered‑choice pension | You must put something in every year (no‑minimum‑balance panic) | 40 % forced annuity at 60 yr, rest locked till 60 (or 15 yr under new MSF) | Up to 75 % equity (now even pure‑equity under MSF) |
| **EPF** | Statutory employee provident fund | 12 % of basic + DA from salary | Can’t touch before 5 yr (or specific life‑event) | ~90 % debt, tiny equity bite |
| **PPF** | Post‑office long‑term savings | ₹500 minimum per year | 15‑yr lock‑in (extendable) | 100 % sovereign debt (government bonds) |
| **Retirement‑oriented MF** | Goal‑based mutual funds (e.g., “Retirement Advantage”) | Usually ₹1,000‑5,000 per SIP | 5‑yr lock‑in (some have no lock) | Mix of equity‑debt, depending on aggressive‑conservative bucket |

The **biggest differentiator** is that NPS can actually be equity‑heavy, while EPF and PPF are basically “debt‑only” clubs handing you 7‑8 % per‑annum (historical average). In a country where inflation sometimes feels like a ninja that silently steals 6‑7 % of your purchasing power each year, you *need* some equity exposure if you want your nest egg to grow faster than the price of a packet of chips.

### How have NPS equity funds actually performed?  

- **10‑year horizon:** Only **1 out of 6** NPS equity funds beat the Nifty‑100 Total Return Index (TRI) benchmark of **14.4 % CAGR**. The other five lagged by up to **1 %**—so they were basically running a marathon with a slight limp.  
- **5‑year horizon (as of 1 Oct 2024):** Nifty‑100 TRI clocked roughly **19 %** CAGR. NPS equity funds delivered **19‑21 %** CAGR, which is *right on the money* but not spectacular. By contrast, the average flexi‑cap mutual fund (a diversified equity basket) posted **≈21 %** CAGR, meaning some mutual funds sprinted ahead while others stumbled.

**Bottom line:** NPS’s overall return will be *lower* than the pure‑equity component because your money is spread across a mix of equity **and** debt. The debt slice acts like a safety net, but it also drags the average down. (Good news: the new **MSF framework** slated for September 2025 will let you slip into pure‑equity NPS schemes, if you like living on the edge.)

### Goal‑based mutual funds for retirement  

These are the “one‑stop‑shop” funds that say, “Hey, we’ll take your money, invest it across a balanced mix, and lock it for at least five years.” They come in three flavors:

| **Risk bucket** | **Equity share** | **Debt share** | **Tax perks** | **Lock‑in** |
|-----------------|------------------|----------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Conservative** | 0‑20 % | 80‑100 % | Up to ₹1.5 L tax deduction under Sec 80C (plus ₹50 k under Sec 80CCD(1B) if NPS‑like) | ≥5 yr |
| **Hybrid** | 20‑60 % | 40‑80 % | Same as above | ≥5 yr |
| **Aggressive** | 60‑100 % | 0‑40 % | Same as above | ≥5 yr |

Because they’re relatively new (most launched after 2015), we don’t have a 20‑year track record to brag about. Think of them as the “new kid on the block” – promising, but still proving their stamina.

### Quick‑look comparison chart  

![NPS Comparison Table](https://zerodha.com/varsity/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/NPS-Comparision-table.png)

---

## 2.2 – Annuity Products  

Annuities are the **“pay‑me‑every‑month”** cousins of retirement plans. You hand a lump sum to an insurer, they promise a steady stream of cash for the rest of your life (or a fixed term).  

**Example:** You give the insurer **₹1 cr** and they say, “We’ll give you **₹7 lakh per year** at a 7 % annuity rate, forever, as long as you’re breathing.” If you kick the bucket, the plan ends—no leftover cash for your heirs (unless you bought a “return‑of‑purchase‑price” rider).

### The many flavors of annuities  

| **Type** | **Capital return?** | **Inflation shield?** | **When do payouts start?** |
|----------|---------------------|-----------------------|----------------------------|
| **Immediate (no‑return)** | No | No | Immediately (or after a short grace period) |
| **Immediate (with‑return)** | Yes (principal back) | No | Same as above |
| **Deferred** | Usually No | Optional | After a pre‑agreed deferral (e.g., 5 yr) |
| **Inflation‑adjusted** | Usually No | Yes (e.g., +5 % per yr) | Immediate or deferred |
| **Joint‑life** | No (or limited) | No | Immediate |

Because the menu is so long, a **straight‑up NPS‑vs‑annuity** head‑to‑head comparison gets messy.  

**But here’s a kicker:** NPS *also* forces you to buy an annuity, but only on **40 % of the corpus** when you hit the retirement age. The remaining 60 % you can park wherever you like (MFs, stocks, real estate, your grandma’s gold collection—you name it).

### What’s the “guaranteed” return on an annuity?  

The industry loves to brag about the *guarantee*, but guarantees usually come with modest yields. Current IRR (internal rate of return) for a typical life‑annuity (monthly payouts from age 60 to ~85‑90) hovers around **7 %** before tax. After accounting for tax on the annuity income, the effective return drops to **≈5.2 %**. If you opt for a version that **returns the principal**, the IRR slides even lower.

Because the returns are modest and the product is less popular for *wealth‑building* (it’s more of a “cash‑flow safety net”), we left annuities out of the big performance tables.  

**Bottom line:** NPS wins on cost‑efficiency and equity exposure, whereas traditional annuities win on absolute payout certainty—but at the price of a lower growth rate.

---

## 2.3 – Should you invest in NPS for retirement?  

The answer isn’t a simple “yes” or “no”—it’s a **“it depends on your vibe”**. Let’s break down the pros and cons like a bartender’s cocktail menu.

### The “cheers” side (pros)

1. **Built‑in discipline** – NPS is the financial equivalent of a *coach’s whistle*. You can’t just waltz to the bank and pull out the whole stash; 40 % must be locked into an annuity at 60, so you won’t end up with a sudden windfall that tempts you to splurge on a golden‑retriever or a yacht you can’t afford.  
2. **Psychological peace of mind** – Knowing you’ll have a guaranteed monthly income (the annuity part) can be a huge stress‑buster. It’s like having a safety net under a tightrope walk.  
3. **Tax sweeteners** – Contributions up to **₹1.5 L** (Sec 80C) plus an extra **₹50 k** under **Sec 80CCD(1B)** are deductible. That’s a solid tax shield while you’re still working.  
4. **Low cost** – NPS charges a tiny fraction (≈0.1‑0.5 % of assets) compared to most mutual funds, leaving more of your money to compound.

### The “watch‑out” side (cons)

| **Con** | **Why it might bite you** |
|---------|---------------------------|
| **Lock‑in** | You can’t touch the money before 60 (or 15 yr under the new MSF). Early retirement? Hard‑ball. |
| **Limited equity cap** | Even if you love stocks, NPS caps equity at 75 % (unless you jump into the upcoming pure‑equity scheme). |
| **Mandatory 40 % annuity** | That chunk goes into a product that yields only ~7 % IRR (≈5 % after tax). Some argue you could get higher returns elsewhere. |
| **Complexity of rules** | New frameworks (MSF, tier‑wise switches) can feel like learning a new board game mid‑match. |
| **Opportunity cost of the debt slice** | The debt portion cushions volatility but drags the overall CAGR down compared to a pure‑equity portfolio. |

### A balanced approach  

Think of NPS as **one pillar** in a diversified retirement house. You could:

1. **Put 30‑40 % of your retirement savings into NPS** – enjoy the tax break and the forced‑annuity safety net.  
2. **Allocate the rest to high‑growth vehicles** – aggressive equity mutual funds, direct stocks, or even a PMS (Portfolio Management Service) if you have the appetite and the cash.  

That way you get the **discipline and tax love** from NPS while keeping the **flexibility** to chase higher returns elsewhere.

### A note for NRIs  

If you’re an NRI, the tax story gets spicier. For instance, the United States may treat NPS holdings as **passive foreign investments**, meaning **unrealized gains** could be taxed each year under the *Passive Foreign Investment Company* (PFIC) rules. That could eat into your returns fast. Always loop in a cross‑border tax advisor before you pour money into an Indian retirement vehicle.

---

## Key Takeaways  

- **NPS is a mixed‑bag** – great discipline, low fees, decent equity exposure, but a mandatory annuity that delivers modest returns.  
- **Features can be love‑or‑hate** – the lock‑in and forced annuity are a blessing for some (peace of mind) and a curse for others (lack of liquidity).  
- **Your comfort with lock‑in matters** – If you’re okay with “I can’t touch this until I’m old and gray,” the product can be a solid foundation.  
- **Diversify** – Use NPS as a *core* retirement pillar, then sprinkle in other investments for growth and flexibility.  
- **Tax and compliance matter** – Especially for NRIs; get professional advice before you dive in.  

In the end, the *choice you make makes you*, as Coach Wooden said. Pick the retirement strategy that matches your risk appetite, life goals, and willingness to let a regulator hold your hand (or your wallet) until you’re ready to retire. Cheers to a financially fit future! 🍻

04_3.title: 3. Investment Options in NPS
04_3.content:
# 3. Investment Options in NPS  

**Source:** https://zerodha.com/varsity/chapter/investment-options-in-nps/  

---  

## 3.1 – Types of accounts  

So you’ve decided to give the National Pension System (NPS) a whirl. First thing’s first – you need to know *how* you’ll get your money in there.  

There are **two highways** you can hop onto:  

| Route | How you get on the road | Who usually drives you |
|-------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **All‑Citizen Model** | You sign‑up yourself, like ordering pizza online. | You – the brave, self‑sufficient investor. |
| **Corporate NPS Model** | Your employer deducts the contribution straight from your salary, like a cafeteria charge. | Your company (if it’s registered under the corporate scheme). |

Under the corporate model the employer can treat NPS as a **mandatory** retirement perk (think “you must have this”) or as a **voluntary** one (think “hey, we’re offering it, you can say no”). If it’s voluntary, you get to decide whether to hop on the pension train or stay on the commuter line.  

Now, once you’re on board, NPS splits your stash into **two account tiers** – Tier I and Tier II.  

* **Tier I** = your *primary* retirement piggy‑bank. Withdrawals are heavily shackled (you can’t just raid it at 25 for a Bali holiday) and you must meet a minimum contribution rule until you hit **70 years** or you retire.  

* **Tier II** = a *savings‑style* account that lives in the same building but has no withdrawal shackles and no mandatory deposit amount. You can only open a Tier II after you already have a Tier I.  

**Minimums & caps** – Tier I needs at least **₹1,000 a year** (or ₹500 per contribution) to stay alive. Tier II? No floor, no ceiling; you can throw in as much or as little as you like.  

In this chapter we’ll keep our eyes on **Tier I**, because that’s the one that decides how fat your retirement cake will be.  

At this stage you have **two big‑brain decisions** to make:  

1. **Asset allocation** – how much of your money goes into equities, how much into bonds, etc.  
2. **Fund manager** – which pension fund will actually do the investing for you.  

Grab a coffee, we’re about to dive deeper.  

---  

## 3.2 – How it works  

Picture a swanky five‑star hotel. You walk in, and—bam!—there are separate restaurants: an Italian trattoria, a sushi bar, a spice‑laden Indian kitchen, a French pâtisserie, and so on. Each spot offers an appetizer, a main, and a dessert.  

Now imagine you’re a picky eater who wants an **appetizer from the sushi bar, a steak from the Italian place, and a tiramisu from the French pâtisserie**. Or, like most of us on a lazy Thursday night, you just order the “Chef’s special” and let the hotel take care of everything.  

NPS works the same way.  

* The **restaurants** are the **pension fund managers** (Axis, HDFC, SBI, etc.).  
* The **dishes** are the **asset classes** (equities, government bonds, corporate debentures, alternatives).  

You can hand the whole menu to one chef (one manager runs everything) **or** you can split the kitchen duties: Chef A handles the equity entrée, Chef B whips up the bond side‑dish, Chef C plates the alternative‑investment dessert. The choice is yours, and you stay the boss of the whole banquet.  

---  

## 3.3 – Investment options  

Under the **All‑Citizen Model** you get to pick from **four “schemes”** – think of them as different cuisine styles.  

| Scheme | What’s on the plate? | Risk & return vibe |
|--------|----------------------|--------------------|
| **Scheme E** (Equities) | Listed shares – mainly large‑cap, a smidge of mid‑cap. | Highest risk, but also the only dish that can really crank up the returns. |
| **Scheme G** (Government securities) | Central‑government bonds, state‑development loans. | Virtually zero default risk, but still sensitive to interest‑rate swings (think of it as a bland soup that can get salty if rates rise). |
| **Scheme C** (Corporate debentures) | Corporate bonds and infrastructure‑linked debt. | Riskier than G, but generally more rewarding than pure cash. |
| **Scheme A** (Alternatives) | AIF‑I/II, REITs, InvITs, Basel‑III Tier‑1 bonds, securitised papers. | The “exotic” menu – higher risk, newer flavours. *(Update: PFRDA’s Dec 13 2025 circular says Scheme A will be merged into Schemes E or C, and gold/silver ETFs are now allowed in Scheme E.)* |

### How much can you load onto each plate?  

* **Scheme E** – up to **75 %** of your NPS basket (unless you go “active choice” and decide otherwise).  
* **Schemes C** and **G** – you can go **100 %** in each, meaning you could theoretically allocate the whole pot to corporate bonds or government bonds if you’re a total risk‑averse soul.  

The regulator (PFRDA) gave the green light in **Oct 2022** to keep equity exposure capped at **75 %** under the “active‑choice” model.  

*(Update: The September 2025 Multiple Scheme Framework (MSF) now lets you invest in pure‑equity funds if you wish – basically a “go‑all‑in” button for the brave.)*  

### Auto‑choice (life‑cycle) funds – the “set‑it‑and‑forget‑it” buffet  

If you don’t want to play portfolio‑tetris, there’s the **auto‑choice** (or “life‑cycle”) option. Your money is automatically re‑balanced as you age, shifting from high‑octane equity to safer bonds as you get older.  

Three flavours are on offer:  

| Code | Name | Aggressiveness | Max equity at age ≤ 35 |
|------|------|----------------|------------------------|
| **LC75** | Aggressive Life‑Cycle | High | **75 %** |
| **LC50** | Moderate Life‑Cycle | Medium | **50 %** |
| **LC25** | Conservative Life‑Cycle | Low | **25 %** |

In **Oct 2024**, a **Balanced Life‑Cycle Fund** entered the menu, tapering equity from **50 %** after you turn **45** (instead of the earlier 35‑year cut‑off).  

If you do nothing, the system will **default you to LC50** (the moderate plan).  

**Pro tip:** Don’t be scared off by the fact that Scheme E may look a bit sluggish in the first few years. Over the long haul equities have historically out‑paced inflation, turning today’s ₹1,000 into a much bigger “future‑you” celebration cake.  

---  

## 3.4 – New schemes under the MSF framework  

September 2025 was a big month: the regulator rolled out the **Multiple Scheme Framework (MSF)**. Think of it as a “chef’s special” menu that lets pension funds launch **theme‑based schemes** – e.g., “High‑Growth for Tech‑Savvy Millennials” or “Stable Income for Freelancers & Homemakers”.  

Each MSF scheme comes in **two risk flavours**:  

* **Moderate** – a balanced plate.  
* **Aggressive** – the “all‑in” platter that can have **up to 100 % equity**.  

The classic **Schemes E, C, G** stay on the roster and are now called **“common schemes.”**  

### What’s different?  

| Feature | MSF schemes | Common schemes |
|---------|------------|----------------|
| **Asset mix** | Pre‑defined (e.g., 80 % equity + 20 % debt) + clear theme | You decide the mix under active or auto choice. |
| **Number you can hold** | Unlimited – stack ‘em like pancakes. | Limited to the three core schemes. |
| **Expense‑ratio cap** | **0.30 %** (a tad higher because they’re more specialized). | **0.09 %** (the lean‑machine fee). |
| **Transferability** | New money only – you can’t move existing common‑scheme balances into an MSF scheme. | Existing balances stay where they are. |
| **Vesting period** | **15 years**. If you start at 30, you can first withdraw at 45 (still subject to the 60/40 split). If you retire before 45, the usual 60 % lump‑sum / 40 % annuity rule applies. | Traditional NPS lock‑in until **60 years** (or earlier under certain conditions). |

So, MSF schemes give you a fresh palette, but they’re only for fresh ingredients – you can’t pour your old stew into the new pot.  

---  

### Visual aid  

![Image](https://zerodha.com/varsity/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/G1DU9eBWoAEWxXs-1.jpg)  

---  

## 3.5 – Selecting fund managers  

The **All‑Citizen Model** currently offers **ten pension fund managers (PFMs)**:  

1. Axis  
2. Aditya Birla Sun Life  
3. HDFC  
4. ICICI  
5. Kotak  
6. LIC  
7. Max Life  
8. SBI  
9. TATA  
10. UTI  

All of them publish performance data online, so you can compare **returns, consistency, and risk metrics** across schemes and time‑frames.  

### Active‑choice multi‑manager magic  

Under the **Active‑Choice** route you may pick **up to three PFMs** and assign each to a different asset class. Example:  

* **Axis** – handles your **Scheme E** (equities).  
* **SBI** – looks after **Scheme C** (corporate debt).  
* **LIC** – stewards **Scheme G** (government securities).  

You can **review and switch** your managers **once per year** (no penalty, no tax event). This is a huge advantage over mutual funds, where moving money from one fund to another is treated as a sale and could trigger **capital‑gains tax**.  

You can also **rebalance your asset allocation** up to **four times a year** (quarterly) if you feel the market’s mood has changed.  

For the nitty‑gritty numbers, the platform provides two handy links (originally embedded in the source):  

* **[Scheme returns]** – a table of historical performance.  
* **[Portfolio details]** – a breakdown of what each manager actually holds.  

---  

### Visual aid  

![Image](https://zerodha.com/varsity/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/NPS-returns-screenshot.png)  

---  

## Key takeaways  

- **Two big decisions** drive your NPS success: **asset allocation** (how much equity vs. debt) and **fund manager** (who actually invests).  
- You have **four scheme choices** (E, G, C, A), but after the 2025 update Scheme A is being merged, and gold/silver ETFs now sit in Scheme E.  
- **Active‑choice** lets you pick the exact percentages (up to 75 % equity). If you’re lazy, the **auto‑choice life‑cycle funds** adjust the mix for you based on age.  
- **Rebalance** your asset mix **four times a year**; you can swap fund managers **once a year**.  
- **No tax consequences** when you switch managers or asset classes – unlike mutual funds where each switch can be a taxable event.  

Now go forth, pick your plate, and let your retirement feast begin! 🍽️🚀

05_4.title: 4.    NPS: Exit & Withdrawals
05_4.content:
# 4.    NPS: Exit & Withdrawals **Source:** https://zerodha.com/varsity/chapter/exit-withdrawals/ --- > “What doesn’t kill you makes you stronger.” > (Except that the NPS can also make your retirement *smarter* – if you play by the rules.) This chapter is the “how‑to‑get‑out‑of‑the‑kitty” guide for a Tier‑I NPS account. Whether you’re gracefully bowing out at the golden age of 60, or you’re an impatient youngster itching to tap the fund early, the same rule‑book applies. We’ll also peek at what happens if the universe decides to collect you before you can say “superannuation”. > **Pro‑tip:** Treat the NPS like a fine wine – let it age to 60 and then enjoy the full flavour. In NPS‑speak that’s called *normal superannuation*. --- ## 4.1. Normal Exit (Superannuation) Rules ### 🎉 Retirement at Age 60 *When you hit the big 6‑0, the NPS says “congratulations, here’s the exit plan”* | What the law says | What you actually get | |-------------------|----------------------| | **≥ 40 % of the corpus** must be used to buy an **annuity** (that’s the “regular pension” part). | You’ll receive a guaranteed monthly (or quarterly, etc.) payout for life. | | **≤ 60 %** can be taken out **as a lump‑sum**. | You get a nice chunk of cash to splurge on a vacation, a second‑hand yacht, or simply a safety net. | #### Tiny‑pot exception If the total corpus is **₹5 lakh or less**, you can take **100 %** as a lump‑sum. Think of it as the “small‑pot” rule – the regulator says “you’re not big enough to force an annuity on you”. > **Update (2024‑25):** The **MSF (Minimum Savings Framework)** introduced by PFRDA lets a scheme have a **15‑year vesting period**. So, if you start at 30, you could technically walk out at **45** – but you must still respect the 60/40 split (60 % cash, 40 % annuity). If you retire *before* the vesting period ends, you can still exit early, just follow the usual 60 %/40 % rule. ### 🏃‍♂️ Keep the party going – continue contributing after 60 - **You’re still working** and don’t need the cash right now? No problem. You may keep feeding the NPS **until age 75**. - All the usual **tax breaks** (₹50 k under Sec 80CCD(1B) + ₹25 k under 80CCD(2) for employer) keep flowing. - The annuity‑purchase rule still applies when you finally decide to cash out at 75. ### ⏳ The “I’m done contributing, but I’ll wait to withdraw” route You can **stop contributions** right after you turn 60 and **delay the withdrawal** until you hit 75. Here’s the menu: 1. **Defer the 60 % lump‑sum** (you can take it later, up to 75). 2. **Defer the mandatory 40 % annuity purchase** – you have a **3‑year grace period** (i.e., you can postpone buying an annuity until you’re 63). 3. **Do both** – keep the cash in the pot and also hold off on the annuity for up to three years. If you **do nothing** at 60, the system automatically assumes you want the “continue‑til‑75” option. And if the idea of a one‑time 60 % payout scares you, you can **phased‑withdraw** the cash in smaller bites any time before 75. At the magical age of 75, the account **closes itself** – no paperwork, no drama. --- ## 4.2. Types of Annuity Plans You *must* allocate **40 %** of your retirement stash to an annuity – there’s no “I’ll keep it all in cash” loophole. Think of an annuity as a pension‑insurance contract that pays you a regular income **for life** (or until the last eligible person dies). There are **15 PFRDA‑empanelled insurers** offering these plans. (You can see the full list on the PFRDA website – it’s a short read, promise.) | Annuity flavour | How the cash flows | Who gets the money when you’re gone? | |-----------------|-------------------|--------------------------------------| | **Annuity for Life** | Fixed pension for you, **nothing** after death. | No survivor benefit – the policy ends. | | **Life Annuity + 100 % to Spouse** | Same pension for you, then **same amount** to your spouse. | Pays till the second‑most‑recent death. | | **Life Annuity + Return of Purchase Price** | Pension for life, **principal returned** to nominee on your death. | Nominee gets the original sum, no further pension. | | **Life Annuity + 100 % to Spouse + Return of Purchase Price** | Pension continues while either you or spouse lives; after both are gone, **principal** goes to nominee. | | **NPS Family Income Plan** | Pension to you → spouse → parents → finally **principal** to nominee/child. | Cascading survivor benefits ending with a lump‑sum return. | ### How much will I get? The annuity amount depends on: 1. **Amount invested** (the 40 % you’re forced to use). 2. **Payment frequency** (monthly, quarterly, half‑yearly, or yearly). 3. **The insurer’s rate** – think of it as the “interest” the insurer offers you for life. As of **October 2024**, the market‑wide **annuity rates** sit roughly between **7.5 % and 9 %** (annualized). The *higher* end usually belongs to **no‑return‑of‑purchase‑price** products, while the *lower* end is for those that promise a refund of the principal. A 0.5 % rate swing can change your monthly cheque by **₹200–₹300**, so shop around! You can run the numbers on any insurer’s **annuity calculator** (links available on their sites). Below is a snapshot of HDFC Life’s offering for a 60‑year‑old under a few plan variants (just for illustration – don’t take it as a recommendation). ![Sample annuity calculator output – HDFC Life (illustrative)](https://zerodha.com/varsity/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/NPS-Annuity-page.png) --- ## 4.3.  Partial or Premature Withdrawal Life happens. If you need cash before the “official retirement party”, the NPS gives you two escape hatches: | Feature | Partial Withdrawal | Premature Exit | |---------|-------------------|----------------| | **When can you use it?** | After **3 years** of being in NPS. | After **5 years** of mandatory contributions. | | **Maximum you can take** | Up to **25 %** of your *contributions* (not the whole corpus). | If corpus > ₹2.5 lakh → **only 20 %** as lump‑sum; the rest **must** become an annuity. If ≤ ₹2.5 lakh → take **100 %** (no annuity needed). | | **Why would the regulator allow it?** | For **specific life events** (see below). | When you decide to quit the NPS entirely before age 60. | | **Frequency** | Up to **3 times** in your whole NPS life. | One‑time exit (you close the account). | ### Partial‑withdrawal triggers (you need a good excuse) - **Higher education** (your kid’s MBA, not just a weekend workshop). - **Marriage** (yes, the wedding of a son/daughter). - **Purchase/Construction of a house** (first home, not a vacation shack). - **Serious medical treatment** (cancer, heart surgery, etc.). ![Partial withdrawal conditions chart – NPS (illustrative)](https://zerodha.com/varsity/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/NPS-withdrawal-conditions.png) You cannot **borrow** against your NPS (as of 2024). Think of it as a “no‑credit‑card” retirement account – you either use it or you don’t. ### Premature exit (the “I quit before 60” route) - **Eligibility:** 5 years of contributions (no shortcuts). - **If your corpus > ₹2.5 lakh:** The regulator forces you to **buy an annuity with at least 80 %** of the amount. You walk away with only **20 %** cash. - **If corpus ≤ ₹2.5 lakh:** You get the **full amount** as a lump sum, no annuity required. In short, the NPS loves to keep money **working for life**, so it only relaxes the rule when the pot is modest. --- ## 4.4.  Unfortunate Death If the subscriber “checks out” before or after turning 60, the **entire corpus** (both the cash part and the annuity‑eligible 40 %) goes to the **nominee(s)** or legal heirs **without any compulsory annuity purchase**. - **You must name a nominee** when you open the Tier‑I (or Tier‑II) account. - **Up to three nominees** can be listed, each with a **percentage allocation** (e.g., 50 % to spouse, 30 % to son, 20 % to daughter). - The nominee can **choose** to keep the money as a lump sum **or** buy an annuity for themselves – it’s optional, not mandatory. --- ## 4.5.  Final Words The exit‑rules we’ve walked through apply to anyone who **joined NPS before turning 60**. If you entered the scheme **between 60 – 70**, the PFRDA has a slightly different playbook – check the official NPS portal for those specifics. Even if you become **totally disabled** (≥ 75 % disability), the NPS still caps you at **25 %** of the corpus for a one‑time withdrawal. Bottom line: **don’t rely on NPS as an emergency fund**. Keep a separate liquid stash (FDs, liquid mutual funds, a good old‑fashioned savings bank) for those “just‑in‑case” moments. --- ## Key Takeaways | Point | What it means for you | |-------|-----------------------| | **Age‑60 options** | 1️⃣ Take up to **60 %** cash, buy annuity with 40 %
2️⃣ Keep contributing **until 75** (if you’re still working)
3️⃣ Stop contributions **but defer** the cash‑out until 75 (with a 3‑year annuity‑purchase grace). | | **Automatic closure** | If you do nothing, the account **shuts itself** at **75**. | | **Partial withdrawals** | Allowed **3 times** for education, marriage, house, or medical reasons – max **25 %** of contributions each time. | | **Premature exit** | Before 60, you must **annuitize 80 %** (if corpus > ₹2.5 lakh) and can take only **20 %** cash. If ≤ ₹2.5 lakh, you can take it all. | | **Death benefit** | Entire corpus goes to the **named nominee(s)**; they may or may not buy an annuity. | | **Nominee rules** | Up to **3 nominees**; allocate percentages when you open the account. | | **Disability rule** | Even with ≥ 75 % disability, you can only tap **25 %** of the corpus. | | **Don’t forget the emergency fund** | NPS is **not** a quick‑cash vehicle. Keep a separate liquid nest‑egg. | Now that you’ve got the exit‑map, treat your NPS like a fine whiskey: let it age, enjoy the sip at 60, and don’t try to gulp it down too early unless you have a *very* good reason. Cheers to a stress‑free retirement!

06_5.title: 5.  NPS Tier II Account
06_5.content:
# 5.  NPS Tier II Account  

**Source:** <https://zerodha.com/varsity/chapter/nps-tier-ii-account/>  

---  

### The “older‑sibling‑vs‑younger‑sibling” story  

Imagine a household with two kids. The elder is the *responsible* one: curfew at 9 pm, chores on a schedule, a college fund that can’t be touched until graduation. The younger? He’s the “free‑spirited” kid who can stay up late, binge‑watch cartoons, and decide on his own breakfast cereal.  

NPS works exactly the same way. **Tier I** is the disciplined elder—lots of rules, lock‑ins, and a sweet tax‑break candy. **Tier II** is the carefree younger sibling—no minimum contribution, no lock‑in, and you can pull the money out whenever you fancy. The trade‑off? The younger one can’t enjoy the elder’s tax‑benefit “allowance.” As the old saying goes, you can’t have your cake and eat it **and** keep it in the fridge forever.

### How you get a Tier II account  

- First you need a Tier I NPS account (the elder sibling has to exist before the younger shows up).  
- Once Tier I is live, you can open a Tier II account with the same portal—just click, click, and boom, you’ve got a second pot to play with.

### Fund choices: E, G, C (and the “active vs auto” drama)  

Tier II gives you three “flavours” of NPS funds:  

| Code | What it eats | Typical vibe |
|------|--------------|--------------|
| **E** | Equities (big‑cap stocks) | “I’m ready for the market roller‑coaster.” |
| **G** | Government securities | “Safe as grandma’s secret pickle recipe.” |
| **C** | Corporate debentures | “Corporate bonds with a dash of risk.” |

You can go **active‑choice**: you pick the exact mix (e.g., 70 % E, 20 % G, 10 % C). Or you can let the system do the heavy lifting with **auto‑choice**: it starts you off with a higher equity tilt and automatically shifts toward bonds as you age, kind of like a diet plan that swaps fries for oatmeal as you hit 50.

### Pick your fund manager, too  

Just like you can have different chefs in different kitchens, you can assign different Pension Fund Managers (PFMs) to each fund. Want HDFC handling your Tier I equity slice? No problem. Feel like trying UTI for the Tier II piece? Go ahead. The two tiers are completely independent, so you can be a “HDFC‑fan‑boy” in Tier I and a “UTI‑enthusiast” in Tier II without breaking any rules.

### Allocation limits: 75 % vs 100 %  

- **Tier I** caps equity exposure at **75 %** of the total corpus (the regulator says “don’t go all‑in on the market”).  
- **Tier II** lifts that ceiling—**you can go 100 % equity** if you’re feeling adventurous. Just remember, 100 % equity means you’ll ride every market wave, not just the gentle ones.

### Withdrawals, transfers, and the “one‑way street”  

- **No lock‑in**: pull out whenever you want, as often as you like. It’s the financial equivalent of a 24/7 coffee shop.  
- **One‑way transfer**: you can shift money **from Tier II to Tier I**, but not the other way around. Think of it as moving from the “playground” to the “study room,” but you can’t go back.  
- **If you close Tier I**: whatever sits in Tier II gets dumped straight into your linked bank account—no drama, no penalties.

![Image](https://zerodha.com/varsity/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/NPS-tier-1-and-Tier-2.png)

### When Tier I lock‑in feels like a prison, Tier II is the parole officer  

If the 60‑year lock‑in of Tier I makes you break out in a cold sweat, but you still like the NPS’s “engineered” retirement vibe, Tier II can be your mental‑discipline hack. You can tell yourself, “I’ll stash cash in Tier II as if it were a pension pot,” even though the law doesn’t force you to keep it there. It’s like pretending you’re on a diet while secretly eating a cheeseburger—except here the “cheeseburger” is a perfectly legitimate investment.

### Is Tier II really a retirement product?  

The *real* power of NPS comes from its strict contribution‑and‑withdrawal rules that force long‑term thinking. Tier II throws those shackles away, turning the account into an **open‑ended mutual‑fund‑style vehicle**. So, while you can earmark it for retirement, it’s not *technically* a retirement‑savings scheme; it’s just another investment option you can throw into your portfolio.

### Let’s break it down: returns, liquidity, safety  

| Dimension | What you get in Tier II |
|-----------|------------------------|
| **Safety** | Depends on the fund you pick. **Scheme E** (equities) carries the same market risk as any large‑cap equity fund. **Scheme C** (corporate debentures) and **Scheme G** (govt securities) invest mainly in **AAA‑rated** bonds, so credit risk is low. However, bond funds face **interest‑rate risk** – when rates rise, bond prices fall. NPS debt funds tend to hold longer‑duration bonds than typical MF peers, making them a tad more volatile. |
| **Liquidity** | Full freedom. Pull out **anytime**, any amount. No 3‑year lock‑in, no exit penalty. |
| **Returns** | **Scheme E** tracks broad market indices (think Nifty 50 or Sensex) – you get market‑average equity returns. **Schemes C & G** have historically out‑performed comparable mutual‑fund debt categories, thanks to the high‑quality bond universe NPS taps into. |

If you’re already scouting gilt funds or corporate‑bond funds for the debt slice of your portfolio, the NPS **C** and **G** schemes are worth a side‑by‑side comparison. For the equity slice, **Scheme E** can be a contender, but the mutual‑fund universe is jam‑packed with strong alternatives, so shop around.

### Tax treatment – the “grey” elephant in the room  

- **No tax deduction** on contributions (unlike Tier I, where you get Section 80CCD(1B) relief).  
- **Gains** are taxed **at your slab rate**, *not* as capital gains. In practice, this mirrors the tax treatment of debt mutual funds.  

Contrast that with **equity mutual funds**, where long‑term gains are taxed at **12.5 %** (plus a 4 % cess), which is generally lower than the slab rates for high‑income earners.  

There’s a **gray area**: some tax practitioners argue Tier II gains should be treated like regular capital gains, but the official guidance is still fuzzy. Bottom line—don’t bank your entire tax‑saving strategy on an ambiguous rule that might get re‑interpreted next fiscal year.

> **Pro‑tip:** Tax policy changes faster than a cat on a laser pointer. Use tax impact as a **factor**, not the *sole* deciding metric.

### The “tax‑planning” myth (or loophole?)  

Because you can move money **from Tier II to Tier I**, and Tier I withdrawals are tax‑free, some folks have tried to treat Tier II as a “tax‑saving conduit”: pump money into Tier II, let it grow, then shift it to Tier I at redemption and withdraw tax‑free.  

In reality, the tax department sees through that trick quickly. The industry chatter suggests this loophole won’t survive a serious audit for long. So, unless you enjoy living on the edge of a tax‑audit cliff, **don’t count on Tier II for tax‑saving wizardry**.

### TL;DR – Quick‑fire bullet recap  

- Tier II mirrors Tier I in structure but **requires no minimum contribution** and **has no lock‑in**.  
- No tax deductions on contributions; gains are taxed at **your income‑tax slab** (similar to debt funds).  
- You can pick **different fund managers** and **different asset allocations** for Tier II, independent of Tier I.  
- Equity exposure can go up to **100 %** in Tier II (vs. 75 % cap in Tier I).  
- Withdrawals are **completely flexible**; you can also **transfer money one‑way** from Tier II to Tier I.  
- Tier II is best thought of as **another investment bucket**—use it alongside mutual funds for retirement or any other financial goal, but don’t rely on it for tax‑saving magic.  

Enjoy the freedom, respect the risks, and remember: **flexibility is a double‑edged sword—use it wisely, and your portfolio will thank you over the long run.**

07_6.title: 6.  NPS Tax Rules & Benefits
07_6.content:
# 6.  NPS Tax Rules & Benefits  

**Source:** <https://zerodha.com/varsity/chapter/nps-tax-rules-benefits/>  

---

### Why Tax Benefits Feel Like a Cheat‑Code  

Let’s be real – the only thing that gets most of us to actually *save* is the promise of paying **less** to the taxman today.  
Think about it: you’re terrified of ending up on a pension‑less island where you have to beg your kids for a cup‑noodle.  Then, BAM! The government says, “Hey, put some money into this retirement pot and we’ll let you keep ₹ 50 000 (or more) in your pocket now.”  
That’s the “instant‑gratification” of the tax break, and it works because the government loves the idea of a crowd of financially‑fit retirees who won’t be knocking on the social‑welfare door.

Just like the dreaded 28 % GST on cigarettes (the government’s way of saying “don’t smoke, you’ll die and we’ll lose tax revenue”), tax **incentives** are the reverse: they’re a carrot, not a stick.  They say, “Invest for your old‑age and we’ll give you a tax‑free slice of the pie.”

All the big‑ticket retirement products – NPS, EPF, PPF – come with these sweeteners, both when you **put money in** and when you **take it out**.  So let’s crack open the tax‑law book and see what the government has baked into NPS.

---

## The Withdrawal Phase – What Happens When You Cash Out?

When you finally hit the “retirement” button (or decide to retire early because you’ve finally saved enough for that Bali villa), the tax treatment is a bit of a mixed bag.

| What you can pull out | Tax impact |
|-----------------------|------------|
| **Up‑to‑60 % of the corpus** (mandatory lump‑sum) | **Zero tax** – the government says, “Enjoy, you earned it!” |
| **The remaining 40 %** (must be bought as an annuity) | Taxed as **regular income** at your applicable slab each year you receive the annuity. |

So the big win is the **lump‑sum** – you get to walk away with a chunk of cash that the taxman can’t touch. The annuity, however, is like a subscription you pay tax on every month.

> **Quick side‑note:** The above applies only to **Tier‑I** withdrawals. Tier‑II is a different beast – you either get taxed at your slab rate or, in some cases, as a capital gain. The law hasn’t spoken clearly yet, so keep that tax‑advisor on speed‑dial.

Premature exits (you know, those “I need cash for a wedding, baby, or emergency” moments) let you pull **up to 20 %** of the corpus. The rulebook is fuzzy, but the consensus is: **it will be taxed** (most likely as regular income). Again, ask a professional before you do the “early‑bird” move.

Partial withdrawals for *marriage, education, house‑construction, etc.* are a happy exception – they’re **tax‑free**.  The government says, “Go on, celebrate life. We won’t tax that.”  

All of this is **independent of whether you’re on the old or new tax regime** – the withdrawal rules stay the same.

---

## The Investment Phase – Three Sections That Make Your Wallet Smile  

When you start feeding money into NPS, the Income Tax Act throws you three handy sections to reduce your taxable income:

| Section | Who can claim? | Max deduction | Regime |
|---------|----------------|---------------|--------|
| **80CCD(1)** | Salaried & Self‑Employed | **10 % of salary (basic + DA) or ₹ 1.5  lakh**, whichever is lower | **Old regime only** |
| **80CCD(1B)** | Same as above | **Additional ₹ 50 000** over and above the ₹ 1.5  lakh ceiling | **Old regime only** |
| **80CCD(2)** | Employees (employer contributions) | **Employer contribution up to 10 % of salary (basic + DA)** in the old regime; **up to 14 %** in the new regime | **Both regimes** |

Let’s break each one down with a sprinkle of humor.

### 80CCD(1) – The “Basic‑Plus‑DA” Discount  

- **What it does:** Lets you deduct **up to 10 % of your basic + DA** (or ₹ 1.5  lakh, whichever is smaller) from your taxable income for *Tier‑I* contributions.  
- **Why it matters:** This deduction sits inside the famous **Section 80C** bucket (the one that also houses life‑insurance premiums, ELSS, PPF, home‑loan principal, etc.). The ₹ 1.5  lakh ceiling is *shared* across all those investments. So if you already max out ELSS at ₹ 1.5  lakh, you can’t claim any extra under 80CCD(1) unless you free up some space.  
- **Self‑Employed twist:** If you run your own show, you can claim **up to 20 % of your gross income** (still capped at ₹ 1.5  lakh total). Think of it as a “boss‑level” bonus for being your own boss.  
- **Regime alert:** Only works under the **old tax regime**. If you’ve switched to the new slab‑only regime, you’ll have to look elsewhere for deductions.

### 80CCD(1B) – The “Extra‑Cheese” Slice  

- **What it does:** Gives you an **additional ₹ 50 000** deduction *on top* of the ₹ 1.5  lakh limit.  
- **When to use it:** When you’ve already squeezed every rupee out of the 80C/80CCD(1) limit and still want more tax relief.  
- **Regime alert:** Again, **old regime only**. The new regime is a “no‑deduction” zone for this particular slice.

### 80CCD(2) – The Employer‑Side “Free‑Lunch”  

- **What it does:** Any contribution your **employer** makes to your NPS Tier‑I is *not* treated as taxable salary, up to a ceiling.  
- **Old regime ceiling:** **10 % of basic + DA**.  
- **New regime ceiling:** **14 % of basic + DA** – the government is a little kinder in the new regime, giving you a higher “tax‑free” band.  

**Example time:**  
Suppose your basic + DA is **₹ 20  lakh** a year.

| Regime | Employer can contribute tax‑free up to |
|--------|----------------------------------------|
| Old    | 10 % → **₹ 2  lakh** |
| New    | 14 % → **₹ 2.8  lakh** |

Anything beyond those caps is added back to your **CTC** and taxed like ordinary salary.

> **Heads‑up:** If the combined employer contributions to **NPS + EPF + Superannuation** breach **₹ 7.5  lakh** in a FY, the excess **gets taxed** in the employee’s hands, regardless of the 80CCD(2) limit. So keep an eye on that total!

---

## Putting It All Together – A Quick Cheat‑Sheet  

- **Investing (Tier‑I) gives you three deduction highways:**  
  1. **80CCD(1):** 10 % of salary (basic + DA) **or** ₹ 1.5  lakh (old regime).  
  2. **80CCD(1B):** Extra ₹ 50 000 on top of the above (old regime).  
  3. **80CCD(2):** Employer’s contribution up to 10 % (old) or 14 % (new) of salary, tax‑free.  

- **Withdrawal (post‑60) looks like this:**  
  - **60 %** of the corpus → **tax‑free lump‑sum**.  
  - Remaining **40 %** → must be bought as an **annuity**; the annuity payouts are taxed at your normal slab each year.  

- **Early exit (≤ 20 % of corpus) and Tier‑II withdrawals** are likely taxable (slab or capital‑gain, depending on the situation).  

- **Partial withdrawals for specific life events** (marriage, kids’ education, house‑building) are **tax‑free** – the government loves happy families.

- **All of the above stays the same** whether you’re on the **old** or **new** tax regime (except the deduction limits for 80CCD(1) and 80CCD(1B), which are old‑regime only).

---

### TL;DR Bullet Recap (just like the original, but with a dash of swagger)

- **NPS Tax Breaks on Investment** – Sections **80CCD(1)**, **80CCD(1B)**, and **80CCD(2)** hand you deductions for Tier‑I contributions.  
- **Section 80CCD(1):** Up to **10 % of salary (basic + DA) or ₹ 1.5  lakh** (whichever is lower) – **old regime only**.  
- **Section 80CCD(1B):** Extra **₹ 50 000** beyond the ₹ 1.5  lakh ceiling – **old regime only**.  
- **Section 80CCD(2):** Employer’s NPS money is tax‑free up to **10 % of salary** (old) or **14 %** (new).  
- **NPS Withdrawal Rules:**  
  - **60 % lump‑sum** at ≥ 60 years → **no tax**.  
  - **40 %** must become an **annuity** → taxed as ordinary income each year you receive it.  

That’s the whole shebang. Save now, enjoy the tax break, and retire like a boss – all while keeping the taxman at arm’s length. 🎉💰🚀

08_7.title: 7.  NPS structure, fees, how to open an account, and start a SIP
08_7.content:
# 7.  NPS structure, fees, how to open an account, and start a SIP  

**Source:** https://zerodha.com/varsity/chapter/nps-structure-fees-how-to-open-an-account-and-start-a-sip/

---

Imagine you’re building a house. You don’t lay the bricks yourself—you hire a plumber, an electrician, an architect, maybe even a guy who brings you coffee when the foreman gets cranky.  
The **National Pension System (NPS)** works the same way: a whole cast of characters hustles behind the curtains so you can simply park your money and let it grow till you’re ready to hang up your work boots.  

When the construction is finished you get a set of blueprints and a key. In NPS you get a **PRAN – Permanent Retirement Account Number**. It’s a 12‑digit, portable, “this‑is‑me‑forever” ID that follows you from your first contribution right up to the day you finally cash out (or decide to keep it alive forever, because who doesn’t love a good pension).  

Below is the backstage crew that makes the NPS show run smoothly.

![Image](https://zerodha.com/varsity/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/NPS-architechture.png)

---

## The one who helps you open an account – **PoP (Point of Presence)**  

| What they do | How they behave | Where you’ll find them |
|--------------|----------------|------------------------|
| First point of contact – think of them as the reception desk for NPS. They take your details, give you a quick tour, and hand you the “welcome kit”. | Banks, financial houses, and some fintechs love the PoP title. They earn a tiny commission every time you toss money into NPS, kind of like a bartender who gets a tip on each round. | Both online portals (your phone, laptop) **and** brick‑and‑mortar branches. Want the full list? Check the **registered PoPs** link on the NPS website. |

> **Pro tip:** If you already have a bank account with a PoP (say, SBI or HDFC), they already have your KYC on file, so the paperwork speeds up like a pizza delivery after you’ve ordered before.

---

## The one that administers transactions & keeps records – **CRA (Central Record Keeping Agency)**  

- **Who are they?** Three big‑brothers: **Protean (formerly NSDL e‑Gov), CAMS, and KFintech**.  
- **What’s their job?** They receive your subscriber info (either straight from you or via the PoP), mint your PRAN, and run the back‑office like a well‑oiled spreadsheet.  
- **What do they track?** Contributions, account statements, periodic updates, and the occasional “hey, we’ve moved your money” notification.  

Think of the CRA as the **accountant** who never sleeps, never drinks coffee, and never forgets a single transaction—because it’s all stored in the cloud.

---

## The one that actually invests your money – **Pension Fund Managers (PFM)**  

- These are the fund‑guy‑guys you pick when you sign up: **SBI Pension Fund, UTI Pension Fund, ICICI Prudential, etc.**  
- Their job: Take the cash you send, split it among **Equity, Debt, and Government Securities** according to the scheme you chose (Active vs Auto) and your risk appetite.  
- In short, they’re the **chefs** turning raw ingredients (your contributions) into a gourmet retirement dish.

---

## The one that moves the cash day‑to‑day – **Trustee Bank**  

- After you hand over money to the PoP/CRA, it lands in the **Trustee Bank** (currently **Axis Bank**).  
- The bank acts like a **courier**: it receives instructions from the CRA, then forwards the funds to the right PFM.  
- No magic, just a very secure, audited, and regulated money‑hand‑off.

---

## The one that safeguards the securities – **Custodian Bank**  

- The PFMs buy stocks, bonds, etc., but the actual ownership lives in the **NPS Trust**.  
- The **Custodian Bank** (today **Deutsche Bank**) holds these securities in **demat** form, makes sure dividends land where they belong, and alerts PFMs about corporate actions (splits, bonus issues, you name it).  
- If the securities were a treasure chest, the custodian is the vault with laser beams and a 24‑hour guard dog.

---

## The one that watches the whole circus – **NPS Trust**  

- This trust is the **ringmaster**: it checks that every performer (PoP, CRA, PFM, banks) follows the script written by the regulator.  
- It monitors assets, audits accounts, and ensures the show stays compliant and transparent.

---

## The regulator that keeps the circus safe – **PFRDA (Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority)**  

- Think of PFRDA as the **safety inspector**. It writes the rulebook, inspects the rides, and makes sure every stakeholder plays nice with the subscriber’s money.  

---

## The fee cocktail – Who charges what?  

![Image](https://zerodha.com/varsity/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/NPS-POP-Charges.png)  
*Source: Compiled from NPS Trust as at the time of publishing this article*

| Entity | Typical fee | Comments |
|--------|------------|----------|
| **CRA** (admin fee) | Fixed, standard across all CRAs | Same whether you go via PoP or directly. |
| **PoP** (distribution commission) | Up to **0.5 %** of each contribution, **₹30** minimum, **₹25,000** maximum per transaction | You pay this every time you top‑up via the PoP. |
| **PFM** (fund management) | ~**0.01 %–0.05 %** of assets under management (AUM) | Usually baked into the scheme’s expense ratio. |
| **Trustee Bank** | Nominal **₹10–₹15** per transaction (often absorbed) | Mostly invisible to you. |
| **Custodian Bank** | Charged to PFMs, not directly to you | Again, indirect. |

**Bottom line:** If you love a hands‑off, DIY vibe, bypass the PoP and stick with the CRA (eNPS). If you enjoy a little guidance (or just want your favorite bank’s logo on the receipt), the PoP’s extra 0.5 % is the price of convenience.

---

## DIY vs. “Let‑Me‑Hold‑Your‑Hand” – Choosing a route  

- **DIY Investor** → Go straight to the CRA (eNPS). No extra commission, you’re the captain of your own ship.  
- **Guided Investor** → Use a PoP. You’ll pay a small tip (commission), but you get personal help, a familiar brand, and maybe a coffee while you fill forms.

It’s the same trade‑off you face with mutual funds: **direct plan** (no commission, you manage) vs. **regular plan** (slightly higher cost, dealer assistance).  

---

## Opening an NPS Account Directly with CRA (eNPS)  

1. **Pick a CRA** – hop onto **KFintech**, **CAMS**, or **Protean** websites.  
2. **Register** – you’ll need:  
   * PAN  
   * Mobile number (the one you actually use, not a landline)  
   * Email ID  
   * Date of birth  
3. **Aadhaar authentication** – after the first step, you’ll be whisked to an Aadhaar‑based login (OTP + biometric if you have a scanner).  
4. **Choose a Pension Fund Manager** – pick your favourite (SBI, UTI, etc.).  
5. **Active vs Auto choice** –  
   * **Active** – you decide the equity‑debt split.  
   * **Auto** – the system picks a glide‑path based on your age (the older you get, the more debt, the younger you get, the more equity).  
6. **Bank details** – the account where contributions will be pulled from.  
7. **FATCA** – If you’re an NRI/PIO in the U.S., you must self‑certify under **Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act**. Think of it as the IRS’s version of “show me your ID”.  
8. **Nomination details** – who inherits the pot if you decide to take a permanent nap early.  
9. **Signature upload** – a scanned signature (or a digital one) is required for legal niceties.  

After you click “Submit” and make your first contribution (Tier 1 or Tier 2), the CRA will generate your **PRAN** in **T+2** days. You’ll receive it via SMS/email.  

**Tip:** Contributions also take T+2 to settle, so you won’t see the money instantly—but that’s the same lag you see with most bank transfers, and it gives the system time to double‑check everything.  

Once you have the PRAN, download the **eNPS app** (or use the CRA’s web portal) to track balances, edit nominations, or set up a SIP. Everything is fully online, no paperwork required unless you love the feel of a pen in hand.

---

## Opening an NPS Account Through a PoP  

1. **Find a PoP** – there are **80,000+** branches across India, from big‑name banks to small fintech kiosks.  
2. **Choose your mode:**  
   * **Fully online** – fill a digital form, upload docs, and wait for the PRAN to land in your inbox.  
   * **Hybrid** – online form + a quick visit to the branch for signature/Aadhaar.  
   * **Fully in‑person** – walk into the branch and let the staff walk you through everything.  
3. **Nomination & KYC** – same as CRA, but if you’re already a customer of that bank, they already have your KYC, so it’s quicker than waiting for a queue at the DMV.  
4. **PRAN generation** – usually faster online; if you do it physically, the branch will let you know via SMS/email once it’s ready.  
5. **Receipts** – you’ll get two: one confirming registration, another confirming the contribution amount.  

**Switching rules:**  
- You can **move from eNPS → PoP** later (just submit a request).  
- **But you can’t go back** from PoP to eNPS. Once you’re in the PoP world, you stay there for that account.  

---

## Setting Up an NPS SIP (Systematic Investment Plan)  

### The classic way – T+2 processing  

Every contribution you make takes **two business days** to reflect. That’s fine most of the time, but if you’re a nervous investor who watches the market like a soap‑opera, you might want faster.

### Enter **D‑Remit (Direct Remittance)**  

- **What is it?** A way to push money *directly* from your bank to your NPS account **without** going through the CRA or PoP portal.  
- **How to use it?**  
  1. **Create a Virtual ID** for NPS (think of it as a secret password for your pension). The steps are on the NPS website.  
  2. **Add the Virtual ID** as a beneficiary in your net‑banking or UPI app.  
  3. **Transfer** any amount whenever you like, or set up a standing instruction for a monthly SIP.  

**Why it’s cool:**  
- **Same‑day NAV** – If the market dips today, your money is allocated at the lower price (provided you hit the cut‑off time). No more “I wish I’d contributed yesterday”.  
- **Zero middle‑man fees** – The PoP commission disappears because you’re sending straight from your bank.  

### SIP mechanics  

- Choose a **monthly amount** (₹500, ₹1,000, etc.).  
- Set the **date** (e.g., 5th of every month).  
- The bank auto‑debts your account and pushes the cash via D‑Remit.  
- You’ll see the units credited *the same day* (subject to the cut‑off).  

---

## What to do when something goes sideways – Grievance redressal  

1. **Start with your PoP** – they have a **Grievance Redressal System**. Call, email, or drop by the branch.  
2. **If the PoP can’t help**, raise a ticket on the **CRA’s website** (each CRA has its own **Central Grievance Management System – CGMS**).  
3. **You’ll receive a token number** – keep it handy; you’ll need it to track the status.  
4. **Resolution time** – the regulator mandates **30 days** for a response.  

If you’re still not satisfied after the first level, you can **escalate** using the matrix below (email IDs for each level are listed on the NPS portal).  

![Image](https://zerodha.com/varsity/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/NPS-pyramid.png)  
*Source: NPS Trust*

---

## TL;DR – The cheat sheet  

- **Players**: PoP, CRA, Pension Fund Managers, Trustee Bank, Custodian Bank, NPS Trust, PFRDA.  
- **Fees**: CRA fees are standard; PoP adds up to **0.5 %** (₹30 – ₹25,000) per contribution.  
- **PRAN**: Your lifelong retirement ID—keep it safe, it’s your passport to the pension world.  
- **Account opening**: Either **directly with a CRA (eNPS)** – no extra commission, or via a **PoP** – extra help, extra cost.  
- **SIP**: Use **D‑Remit** + Virtual ID for same‑day allocation and zero middle‑man fees.  
- **Complaints**: Lodge with PoP first, then CRA’s CGMS; expect a 30‑day turnaround; escalation matrix available if needed.  

Now you’ve got the whole NPS crew, their fees, the PRAN magic trick, and the shortcut to automate contributions—all served with a side of humor. Go ahead, open that account, set the SIP, and let your retirement savings grow while you’re still busy figuring out what to have for dinner. Cheers! 🍻

09_8.title: 8. NPS Vatsalya
09_8.content:
# 8. NPS Vatsalya  

**Source:** https://zerodha.com/varsity/chapter/nps-vatsalya/  

---  

### The “Parent‑to‑Kid Retirement” Idea  

Indian parents have a reputation for pulling all‑nighters, juggling three jobs, and still managing to get you that brand‑new bike on your birthday. NPS Vatsalya is basically that same “I‑will‑make‑sure‑you‑don’t‑starve‑when‑you‑’re‑old” vibe, but wrapped up in a tidy retirement‑account format.  

Think of it as a **retirement piggy‑bank** that lives in your child’s name, while you (the guardian) keep the keys and feed it regular contributions until the kid finally turns the legal adult age of 18.  

> **TL;DR:** It works exactly like a regular NPS Tier‑1 account, just with a minor as the beneficiary and a guardian pulling the strings.  

---

## How It Fits Into the NPS Family Tree  

If you missed the “elder sibling vs. carefree younger sibling” analogy we used for Tier I and Tier II, here’s a quick refresher:  

| NPS Tier | Personality | Restrictions |
|----------|-------------|--------------|
| **Tier I** | The responsible elder who always follows the house rules | Lots of lock‑ins, limited withdrawals |
| **Tier II** | The carefree younger sibling who can go wherever they want | Almost no lock‑ins, high flexibility |

Now meet the **newest kid on the block – NPS Vatsalya**. It’s the **youngest sibling**, but it inherits most of the elder sibling’s discipline (i.e., Tier I rules).  

- **Minimum contribution:** ₹1,000 per year (yes, that’s the same as Tier I).  
- **Access:** Only in a handful of “emergency‑only” situations.  
- **Age‑18 switch‑over:** At 18, the account magically becomes a regular NPS Tier I – *sans* guardian. The beneficiary can then either stay in the scheme or cash out by buying an annuity.  

---

## Getting the Ball Rolling – Where to Go?  

1. **Find a Point‑of‑Presence (POP) or a Central Record‑Keeping Agency (CRA).** Think of POP as the “cash‑register” for your NPS wish‑list.  
2. **Pick a Pension Fund Manager (PFM).** This is the “coach” who’ll decide how your money does push‑ups.  
3. **Choose your investment style:**  
   * **Active Choice** – you pick the exact mix of schemes.  
   * **Auto Choice** – you let the system decide (we’ll talk about the three preset flavors later).  

---

## Who Can Sign Up?  

| Who? | Requirement |
|------|-------------|
| **Minor (the beneficiary)** | Must be an Indian citizen (passport, birth certificate, etc.). |
| **Guardian** | Can be an NRI or OCI – no need to be physically in India. |
| **Guardian’s paperwork** | Separate form for NRIs/OCIs, plus a **bank account** (NRE/NRO) if they’re non‑resident. |

*In short: the kid has to be Indian; the adult can be anywhere with the right docs.*  

---

## Investment Flavors – Auto‑Choice Menu  

Just like your favorite ice‑cream shop, NPS Vatsalya offers three “pre‑set” portfolios (the Auto‑Choice).  

| Flavor | Equity Exposure | What the rest looks like |
|--------|----------------|--------------------------|
| **Conservative** | 25 % | 75 % split between government securities & corporate bonds. |
| **Moderate** *(default)* | 50 % | Same split for the remaining 50 %. |
| **Aggressive** | 75 % | 25 % in safe‑haven bonds & gov’t paper. |

If you’re the **Active‑Choice** fan, you can push the equity needle up to **75 % in Scheme E** and **100 % in Schemes C & G** (the corporate and government bond schemes). The only cap is on **Scheme A** (the ultra‑conservative one) – you can’t put more than **5 %** there.  

---

## Money‑In, Money‑Out – The Numbers  

| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| **Minimum annual contribution** | ₹1,000 (same as Tier I). |
| **Opening contribution for a minor** | ₹1,000 (for an adult it’s just ₹500). |
| **PRAN (Permanent Retirement Account Number)** | Issued in the minor’s name – think of it as their lifelong “bank‑ID”. |

### Docs You’ll Need  

| Document | Who Needs It? | What It Looks Like |
|----------|----------------|--------------------|
| **Proof of DOB (minor)** | Child | Birth certificate, school leaving cert., matriculation, PAN, or passport. |
| **Guardian KYC** | Adult | Aadhaar, passport, Voter ID, etc. |
| **Guardian PAN** | Adult | PAN card or Form 60 if they don’t have one. |
| **NRE/NRO Bank Account** | NRI/OCI guardian | Either a solo account or a joint one with the minor. |

---

## The Big 18‑Year‑Old Transition  

When the kid hits **18**, a few things happen:  

1. **Account flips to a regular NPS Tier I (All‑Citizen Model).**  
2. **Fresh KYC** – the new adult must update their KYC within **3 months** of turning 18.  
3. **Guardian becomes the nominee** (so the money still has a fallback).  
4. **All Tier I rules apply** – you can’t suddenly pull out the whole stash; you must follow the normal exit norms (mostly annuity purchase).  

---

## “Oops, I Need Money” – Partial Withdrawals  

Life throws curveballs, and NPS Vatsalya lets you duck a few of them **after the first three years** of the account’s life. You can withdraw **up to 25 % of the *contributions*** (note: *not* the returns) for the following reasons:  

- **Education** of the minor (think tuition, books, or that pricey coding bootcamp).  
- **Treatment of specified illnesses** (hospital bills that make you wish you’d invested in a health‑insurance unicorn).  
- **Disability ≥ 75 %** (serious, life‑changing impairment).  

**Limits:**  

- **Maximum three partial withdrawals** before the child turns 18.  
- **Lock‑in:** Minimum 3‑year wait from account opening before the first withdrawal.  

---

## Tax Perks – Because Everybody Loves a Good Deduction  

- **Section 80CCD(1B)** allows a **₹50,000** deduction for contributions to NPS Tier I *or* NPS Vatsalya (the two share the same ceiling).  
- **Budget 2025** extended this benefit to Vatsalya, effective **FY 25 (April 1 2025)**.  
- **Only under the old tax regime** – if you’re on the new regime, you won’t get this specific deduction.  

### What Happens If You Cash Out Early?  

- **Tax again** – any amount you pull out after enjoying the deduction gets taxed (the “you‑can’t‑have‑your‑cake‑and‑eat‑it‑twice” rule).  
- **No tax on death** – if the beneficiary tragically passes away, the amount isn’t taxed.  
- **Partial withdrawals for education/illness** are **tax‑free for parents**, as long as they stay within the 25 % contribution limit.  

---

## Why Was This Product Cooked Up?  

| Goal | What It Looks Like in Real Life |
|------|---------------------------------|
| **Instill a savings habit early** | A dedicated account in the child’s name that you talk about at bedtime (“Hey, your retirement fund grew by 3 % today!”). |
| **Secure their retirement** | The money is locked away for the long haul, so they’ll have a nest egg when they’re 60‑plus. |

> *Proverb alert:* “Give a man a fish, and you feed him for a day; teach a man to fish, and you feed him for a lifetime.”  
>  
> With NPS Vatsalya you’re doing both – you’re giving them a fish (the money) **and** teaching them to fish (the habit of saving for retirement).  

The scheme shines for families with **special‑needs children**, who need a guaranteed, long‑term financial cushion. For the average family, it’s a *nice* tool but not a *must‑have*.

---

## The “Should‑I‑Do‑It?” Checklist  

1. **Secure your own financial base first.**  
   - Your retirement, home loan, emergency fund, and your kid’s education should be in order before you think about their retirement.  
2. **Know the withdrawal caps.**  
   - Only **25 % of contributions** can be used for education, and you get just **three chances** before the kid turns 18.  
3. **Equity ceiling is 75 %** – not a wild‑growth rocket, but decent for a 15‑year horizon.  
4. **At 18, the kid can bail.**  
   - If they hate NPS, they can exit, but **80 % of the corpus must be used to buy an annuity** (so they can’t walk away with the whole pot).  

### Quick‑Hit Summary  

- **NPS Vatsalya = NPS Tier I for minors** (guardian‑managed).  
- **₹1,000 minimum per year**, PRAN issued in child’s name.  
- **Three auto‑choice portfolios** (Conservative 25 % equity, Moderate 50 % equity, Aggressive 75 % equity).  
- **Active Choice caps:** up to 75 % in Scheme E, 100 % in Schemes C & G, max 5 % in Scheme A.  
- **Eligibility:** Indian minor; guardian can be NRI/OCI with proper paperwork and NRE/NRO account.  
- **Partial withdrawals** (education, illness, severe disability) allowed after 3 years, up to 25 % of contributions, max 3 times before 18.  
- **Tax deduction**: ₹50,000 under 80CCD(1B) (shared with Tier I) – old‑regime only, from FY 25.  
- **At 18** → transforms into a regular Tier I; fresh KYC needed; 80 % of corpus must go to annuity if they exit early.  

---

### Final Thought – “Do It If It Makes Sense”  

NPS Vatsalya can be a **great classroom** for teaching kids about long‑term saving, and it gives parents a tax‑friendly way to start the habit early. But remember: **personal finance is personal**. If you’re already juggling your own retirement, mortgage, and that dream Bali vacation, make sure those priorities are sorted before you lock money away for someone else’s retirement.  

Happy saving, and may your kids grow up financially wiser than you were at their age! 🚀💰

10_9.title: 9.                    Corporate NPS
10_9.content:
# 9.                    Corporate NPS  

**Source:** https://zerodha.com/varsity/chapter/corporate-nps/  

---  

A buddy pinged me the other day: “My company just rolled out corporate NPS. Should I hop on the bandwagon or stick with my regular savings?”  

The National Pension Scheme (NPS) has been climbing the popularity charts like a chart‑topping Bollywood number. In plain English, it’s a retirement‑savings pot where you lock away a chunk of your paycheck during your working years so you can sip a cocktail on the beach when you’re old‑and‑gray.  

**Budget 2024** gave corporate NPS a shiny new coat of tax‑saving paint by letting you claim a deduction on **employer contributions** (up to 14 % of basic + DA) **under the new tax regime**.  

Quick reality check: the *new* tax regime **does not** give any break for “individual” NPS contributions you make on your own. So, fun‑fact: routing the contribution through your employer is the legal‑ese way of getting a tax break.  

A handful of firms already have corporate NPS on their benefits menu, and a growing number are eyeing it like a kid eyeing the last slice of pizza.  

If you’re punching the clock in the private sector, here’s the low‑down before you click “yes”.  

---  

## EPF – the old faithful  

You know EPF, right? The **Employees’ Provident Fund Scheme**.  

* **You** and **your employer** each toss 12 % of your basic + DA into the EPF pot every month.  
* It’s mandatory—think of it as the “no‑opt‑out” version of a gym membership.  
* Your slice shows up as a deduction on your payslip; the employer’s slice is baked into your **CTC (cost‑to‑company)**, so you don’t see a separate line item.  

That’s as straightforward as a plain dosa.  

---  

## Corporate NPS – the fancy cousin  

Corporate NPS works a little differently. When you say “I’m in”, the so‑called “**employer contribution**” (usually 10 % or 14 % of basic, depending on the policy) is **actually taken out of your gross salary**.  

1. The company **deducts** that amount from your pay.
